text: The video shows the previous mesh, identified as a 3D Max mid, extra large, positioned slightly too high, contributing to the posterior recurrence of the hernia. Retroperitoneal fat is visible, suspected to have been left behind from previous operations.

The surgeon uses a fourth robotic arm to explore the area, attempting to identify a plane between the peritoneum and the mesh. The mesh appears to be placed in the preperitoneal space, outside the retro-rectal space, which is easily dissected.

The surgeon observes a folded mesh protruding slightly, indicating its anterior placement. The surgical field reveals multiple layers laterally, allowing for the identification of a plane without cutting through the transverse abdominus muscle.

The surgeon performs dissection in a more anterior plane than previous attempts, avoiding injury to the inferior epigastric vessels by applying downward traction. The surgical field is wide open, allowing for aggressive and quick movements, with the fourth robotic arm aiding in retraction and manipulation of the mesh.

The surgeon works medially, anterior to the mesh, ensuring the bladder remains dorsal to the mesh to avoid injury. Using tension and counter-tension, the surgeon carefully divides tissue layers while maintaining hemostasis for clear visibility of important structures. The dissection progresses smoothly, with the inferior epigastric vessels successfully separated.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect deeper into the lateral portion, which is open due to the mesh not extending laterally or posteriorly. The dissection begins by identifying normal tissue planes and moving towards the scar tissue. The surgeon attempts to locate the testicular vessels early, but notes the presence of significant retroperitoneal fat, making the vessels difficult to find.

The surgeon identifies the blue line from the 3D Max mesh, indicating the presence of additional mesh distal to this line. They proceed to work broadly with the soft tissue, being cautious of the nearby bladder. The surgeon carefully navigates the area, utilizing the abundant areolar tissue for dissection.

The surgeon moves from a deeper dissection to a more superficial area, focusing on finding the plane along the anterior portion of the mesh. The dissection is performed cautiously to avoid injuring the inferior epigastric vessels, with visible fat and the transverse saliv sling medially. The surgeon uses cautery aggressively along the broad plane, ensuring no nerves are injured.

The surgeon identifies a blue line on the mesh, indicating the amount of mesh left to dissect. The blue line is positioned about a third of the way from the posterior edge. The surgeon navigates laterally, entering a plane where the testicular vessel fat and peritoneum become visible.

The surgeon identifies the posterior lateral edge of the mesh and begins peeling the fatty tissue off the peritoneum. A microfacial layer, referred to as the Shiozaki layer, is visible, which helps separate the testicular vessels from the peritoneum within the retroperitoneal fat. The surgeon works on this layer to remove the mesh while preserving the testicular vessels.

The surgeon attempts to separate the retroperitoneal fat from the peritoneum, following the edge of the mesh. The mesh is lifted off as the plane between the mesh and the testicular vessels appears benign, allowing for easy separation. The surgeon navigates through multiple layers of fatty tissue, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid injuring the vas deferens.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the mesh, mindful of the proximity to the vas deferens and testicular vessels. The mesh is peeled off the testicular vessels, revealing multiple layers of tissue. The surgeon considers removing a significant portion of the mesh, ensuring minimal damage to the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon encounters a sticky section of mesh and inadvertently creates a small hole in the peritoneum. The dissection plane becomes challenging, prompting a shift in focus to separate the mesh from the inferior epigastric vessels, vas deferens, and iliac vein, aiming to remove the posterior middle portion of the mesh.

The surgeon carefully inspects the bottom edge of the mesh, ensuring the vas deferens is not visible on the mesh. The surrounding tissue is soft and easy to work with, allowing for good visualization. The surgeon employs a meticulous hemostatic technique while continuing to search for the vas deferens, which is not yet visible.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the mesh using a benign adhesive tissue plane, ensuring not to damage the vas deferens. The mesh is visibly coming off, revealing a safer space for further dissection. Fatty tissue is observed posteriorly, indicating the potential location of the vas deferens.

The surgeon navigates through healthy fatty tissue, extending the opening medially to improve retraction. As the dissection progresses deeper, attention is given to avoiding the external iliac vein. The surgeon successfully enters the right retro-rectal space, encountering some scar tissue in the middle.

The surgeon observes that the previous mesh placement crossed the midline and extended almost two centimeters into the retropubic space. The mesh appears to have been placed anteriorly or migrated anteriorly. An indirect hernia sac is visible with a knuckle present. The surgeon uses the fourth arm for traction, allowing the left hand to provide counter traction, maintaining tension on the tissue. As the tissue is scored, it breaks apart to reveal the next layer.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the superior pubic ramus, using scissors to feel the bony structure and staying close to it. The mesh is released from Cooper's ligament, revealing softened tissue and reduced inflammatory adhesions. The mesh is peeled away from critical structures, with potential adherence remaining to the vas deferens and bladder.

The surgeon carefully traces the peritoneum at the internal ring, identifying a knuckle of peritoneum and peeling away any fat from its surface. Despite scarring, the surgeon uses traction and counter traction to define the distal edge of the peritoneum, while being cautious of the vas deferens.

The surgeon encounters a large mass of fatty tissue medially, where the vas deferens is expected to be located. Laterally, there is still a significant amount of tissue. The surgeon explores the area, noting a vessel crossing the field and the herniation through the internal ring into the inguinal canal. The peritoneum is visible, and the surgeon begins peeling it back further. Firefly imaging is requested to help identify the testicular vessels and the vas deferens.

The surgeon continues to work on identifying the edge of the peritoneum, which appears scarred. Despite the scarring, the peritoneal plane is visible and being carefully dissected. There is a significant amount of fat on the opposite side of the peritoneum, and the testicular vessels are not at risk of injury. The vas deferens is not yet visible but is expected to be located medially.

The surgeon carefully dissects the posterior part of the mesh away from the bladder, observing a tubular structure coming from the internal ring area towards the mesh. The surgeon notes the unexpected trajectory of the structure, which may be the vas deferens, and continues to separate the mesh from the surrounding areolar tissue.

The surgeon has mobilized approximately half of the mesh, revealing the inferior edge tucked into the retropubic space with the bladder visible underneath. The surrounding fat appears dense and adherent. Two tubular structures are observed, with one suspected to be the left medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon is cautious about dividing the ligament without identifying the vas deferens and other structures. A large flap of mesh obstructs the view, and the surgeon considers transecting the mesh to remove the lateral half while leaving the medial half attached to the bladder and peritoneum.

The surgeon attempts further mobilization of the mesh, encountering increasingly sticky tissue planes. The tubular structure is assessed to determine if it is the vas deferens, with the goal of separating it from the mesh. The mesh is transected to avoid further removal from the bladder, as the tissue becomes more adherent.

The surgeon uses scissors and monopolar energy to divide the mesh, carefully working along the mobilized edge. The mesh is large and cannot be removed through an eight-millimeter port, leading to the decision to orange peel the mesh for extraction.

The surgeon continues to explore the surgical field, identifying and dividing tissue layers that appear as extra fat and micro fascia due to the patient's significant weight loss. The peritoneum is peeled back, revealing underlying structures, while ensuring the vas deferens is not present in the divided tissue. A tubular structure, likely the medial umbilical ligament, is left intact for further examination.

The surgeon carefully peels back the edge of the peritoneum to prevent it from slipping under the mesh to be placed later. The vas deferens is not yet visible, prompting the surgeon to closely follow the peritoneum, which is intimately associated with the vas in this region. The dissection reveals a pocket of tissue resembling peritoneum, but it remains intact. Indocyanine green (ICG) highlights the testicular vessels, making the vas deferens and the plane between the peritoneum and vas clearly visible.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and begins addressing the herniated fat protruding through the internal ring. The surgeon carefully separates the fat from the testicular vessels using Firefly imaging to locate the larger vessels. Thick fascial-like tissue layers are dissected to access and reduce the herniated fat.

Using the Firefly imaging system, the surgeon identifies the testicular vessels and carefully dissects the surrounding microfacial plane. The fat is separated from the plane enveloping the testicular vessels, aided by tension and counter-tension techniques. The open book technique is employed to stretch and visualize tissue layers, ensuring a clear landing zone for mesh placement. The dissection is performed under Firefly to distinguish perfused testicular vessels from non-perfused tissue.

The surgeon controls the scope and grasper to position a 16x12 Progrip flat sheet mesh in the retro pubic space, trimming the upper outer corner for a flat lay. The mesh is tucked behind retroperitoneal fat, providing excellent coverage for the large hernia defect. Two sutures are placed to secure the mesh, one to Cooper's ligament and another closer to the femoral space. The flap is closed using two 6-inch sutures, and a suction cannula is inserted to evacuate the pocket, ensuring proper mesh placement.
